body_txt: Works heavy on speculative elements, such as Science Fiction and Fantasy , often include an assortment of fantastic intangibles which no one could ever encounter in Real Life , yet these will act in a completely consistent way, as if governed by imaginary rules of physics — or at least, they will if the writer knows what they're doing. No matter how fantastic the events in a piece of fiction, Internal Consistency is what makes or breaks the Willing Suspension of Disbelief . You can have the tech guy of La Résistance explain in oblique terms involving the word "nano" why The Empire 's fairy dust superweapon needs an hour to recharge after it fires, and the audience will nod and read on. But if you later have that fairy dust superweapon fire twice in one hour, the audience will start to lose patience with you. Have it fire twice in five minutes , and they'll ask you all the questions they graciously withheld at the beginning, like "Do you even know what you're doing, you hack?" In other words, what makes your supernatural abilities believable is not how well you explain them, but how consistently you portray them. Letting the audience work out the 'rules' for themselves is often preferable to dropping in an Infodump of quasi-physics and pseudo-science . The exception is when you are working with more than one system of magic , in which case you will have to explain how each one works, how they differ from each other, and why one character can or can't use both. Critics may object that the fantasy genre thumbs its nose at consistency; once you've introduced magic, all bets are off. But even characters who aren't constrained by the normal laws of physics are still bound by the laws of narrative . When Maegyar the Chronomancer stops time, he should have both external and internal limits on the use of his power, and his time-stopping abilities should be the setup or the payoff to something within the plot. Waving off a continuity error with "Oh, Maegyar stopped time for that part " will in no way satisfy the audience, who want Maegyar to be a person, not a plot device. It is possible to break consistency without damaging the Willing Suspension of Disbelief . It takes work; basically, the work of making a believable case that the violation did not happen out of nowhere. One way is to have the characters themselves notice the inconsistency ; this only reinforces that it is unusual and there might be an explanation for it somewhere . If none ever ends up being offered, at least it reduces a glaring Plot Hole to mere Fridge Logic . Conversely, overly strict adherence towards these manufactured rules can cause its own problems with stagnation or complexity to the point you lose its functionality . After all, if Earth Magic is not too far off , say, geology , then it's obviously possible to revise a theory or two when new evidence crops up. Related to Wrong Context Magic , when someone explicitly has the ability to circumvent it, Unequal Rites , where there's more than one type of magic, each with their own rules, Racially Exclusive Magic , for when some magic is exclusive to specific races, Otherworld-Exclusive Superpowers , when a character only has powers in an otherworld, and Your Magic's No Good Here , where the rules change in a different dimension or world. See also Beyond the Impossible , where internal logic is deliberately broken. If the rules are the same as the rules governing real physics but the energy source and methods are different that's Scientifically Understandable Sorcery . See also Minovsky Physics , which is a fictional science with detailed laws that makes it feel real and help justify the foundations of the setting. Contrast New Rules as the Plot Demands and How Unscientific! ; also contrast Gameplay and Story Segregation , which is an entire category of notoriously common Video Game violations of this trope. Beware the Voodoo Shark , which appears when your attempts to explain your magic system just raise more questions...
